topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed on my monitor, a stark, unforgiving landscape of numbers. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest beneath my weight. It was supposed to be a routine audit, nothing more, but then I saw the final column, the one detailing quarterly compensation. My eyes snagged on a figure that seemed impossibly large, a number that belonged to someone else entirely. A slow, cold tightening began in my chest, spreading outward until my jaw felt locked. I stared at the screen, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. It was Sarah. The new intern, barely out of college, who consistently outperformed everyone, including me, in terms of perceived value. A low, involuntary snort escaped my lips. I minimized the window, the action feeling heavy and deliberate. I picked up my lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic digging into my palm, and took a long, scalding sip, the heat doing nothing to warm the sudden chill that had settled deep in my core.

[story 2]

I watched Mark from across the cubicle farm, his easy confidence radiating even across the partition. He was talking animatedly with management, his voice carrying a certain polished resonance that always made me feel like a poorly rendered shadow. When the HR report landed in my inbox, the reality hit with the force of a physical blow. His salary, juxtaposed against my own steady, grinding income, was astronomical. My hands started to tremble, a slight, uncontrollable tremor that I tried to mask by gripping the edge of my desk so tightly my knuckles bleached white. I didn't look at the report again; instead, I focused on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunlight slicing through the blinds. A sharp, involuntary exhale escaped me—a sound devoid of any real air—and I started meticulously straightening a stack of papers, the movements stiff, precise, and utterly devoid of any preceding enthusiasm.

[story 3]

The office hummed with its usual monotonous drone, a sound that usually faded into the background noise of my daily existence. Today, however, it felt amplified, grating against my nerves. I found myself staring at the employee directory, a pointless exercise, yet compelled. When I located the entry for David, the junior analyst, the discrepancy between his listed remuneration and my own paycheck presented itself with brutal clarity. A strange, hollow sensation settled behind my eyes, like a sudden vacuum. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wheels on the carpet sounding deafening in the sudden silence of my own head. I walked towards the breakroom, my steps measured and heavy, every movement imbued with a profound sense of resentment that I desperately tried to contain behind a façade of professional neutrality.
